categories.name: Resume / CV Basics
categories.description: Clear, practical guides for writing a CV or resume that is easy to read, honest, and ready to tailor for a specific role.

categories.name: Job-Specific CV Tailoring
categories.description: Advice for turning one solid CV into a focused application, including NHS, medical, and role-specific examples.

categories.name: ATS-Friendly Resume Optimization
categories.description: Plain-English ATS guidance for clean formatting, relevant keywords, and readable CVs that still make sense to recruiters.

categories.name: Examples, Templates & Checklists
categories.description: Templates, checklists, and before-and-after examples for checking whether a CV is ready to send.

articles.title: How to Write a Resume That Recruiters Can Read Quickly
articles.description: A plain-spoken guide to writing a resume that gets read: what to keep, what to cut, and how to make each section useful before you tailor it.

body.text: A good resume does not need to tell your whole work story. It needs to help a tired recruiter understand, in a minute or two, what you do well and why it fits this role. When that answer is buried, good experience can look ordinary.

Do the naming question quickly. If you are unsure whether to call it a CV or resume, use the employer's wording and move on. The real improvement comes from comparing the document with the job description, then giving the finished version one final checklist read before you send it.

body.heading: Turn a clear base CV into a targeted one.
body.text: Upload your CV, paste a job description, and choose which real experience to emphasize.
Put the easy decisions first

Most resumes do not need a clever structure. Start with contact details, a short summary, skills, work experience, education, and then certifications or projects if they matter. Recent graduates can move education higher. Career changers can add a small project section before older experience if it makes the fit easier to see.

items: Keep contact details as real text, not only icons or an image.
Use section headings a recruiter expects: Experience, Education, Skills.
Put the strongest and most relevant information in the top half of the first page.
Remove anything that makes the reader work to understand your fit.

body.text: Write the summary after the rest

Do not start by writing a grand summary. Finish the experience section first, then write two or three lines that pull from the strongest proof on the page. A summary feels more believable when it sounds like a conclusion, not a pitch.

rows: Hardworking professional with excellent communication skills.
Customer support lead with 4 years of SaaS experience, focused on onboarding, retention, and support team quality.
Experienced nurse looking for a new challenge.
NMC-registered nurse with ward and triage experience, confident with patient documentation, escalation, and multidisciplinary handovers.

body.text: Make bullet points prove something

A useful bullet point usually answers three small questions: what did you do, how did you do it, and what changed or mattered? You do not need a number in every line. You do need enough scope for the reader to picture the work.

items: Managed weekly reporting for 12 client accounts, reducing missed follow-ups by standardizing the handover checklist.
Triaged patient calls during peak clinic hours and escalated urgent cases using local protocol.
Rebuilt onboarding emails for new users, cutting repeated setup questions from the support queue.

body.text: Cut the filler
Phrases like responsible for, assisted with, passionate about, and results-driven often hide the real work. If a sentence could sit on almost anyone's resume, make it more specific or cut it.
Keep a base version, then tailor

Keep one base resume that is complete enough to trust. It can be a little longer than the version you send, because it is your working copy. For each application, choose the evidence that belongs in front of that employer and leave the rest in your notes.
Only after the content works should you worry about layout. A calm ATS-friendly resume format keeps the same evidence easy for software to parse and easy for a recruiter to skim.

articles.title: CV vs Resume: What to Send and What Actually Matters
articles.description: Understand the difference between a CV and a resume, when each term is used, and how to choose the right format for a job application.

body.text: The CV vs resume question feels bigger than it is because the words change by country, profession, and employer. The label matters, but it is not a quality signal. The stronger document is the one that gives the reader the right evidence quickly.
Make the first page useful enough that the reader wants to keep going.
For most applications, the naming question is less important than the evidence. A broad, unfinished document needs clearer resume writing before local terminology will make much difference.
The short difference

rows: Resume
Usually used in the US and Canada for most non-academic jobs.
Keep it focused, often one or two pages.
CV
Common in the UK, Europe, healthcare, academia, and research.
Match local expectations; academic CVs can be much longer.
Application CV
What many UK and international employers mean by CV.
Treat it like a focused resume, not a complete career archive.

body.text: When to use a resume

Use a resume for most private-sector roles in the US or Canada. Keep it concise, job-specific, and easy to scan. Two clear pages are better than one crowded page, but every section should earn its place.
When to use a CV

Use a CV when the employer asks for one, especially in the UK, Europe, healthcare, education, academia, and research settings. Extra detail is not automatically padding. For NHS and medical roles, the reader may expect registration, clinical experience, audits, teaching, publications, and courses.
Medical applications are the obvious exception to the short-resume instinct. The medical CV guide and NHS medical CV guide show which extra sections help the reader judge your fit.
What matters more than the label

Length is only a clue. A one-page resume can still be weak if it is vague, and a three-page CV can be useful if the role genuinely needs clinical rotations, publications, audits, or research detail. Let the employer, country, and profession decide how much detail belongs in the file, then edit for relevance within those limits.

items: Use the same wording as the job ad when it is accurate for your experience.
Put recent, relevant evidence before older detail.
Keep formatting simple enough for recruiters and ATS software.
Avoid sending one generic document to every employer.

body.text: A simple decision rule

Let the document name come from the closest source. If the application portal, recruiter, or job ad gives a name, use that name. If it does not, use the local convention: resume for most US private-sector roles, CV for UK and many international applications. Then move on.

The useful work starts once the label is settled. The same CV becomes a stronger application when the summary, skills, and first bullets are matched to the job description instead of left as a general career summary.

body.heading: Not sure which version to send?
body.text: Start with your full CV, then create a focused version for the job description.

articles.title: How to Tailor Your CV to a Job Description Without Overdoing It
articles.description: A practical way to compare your CV with a job description, choose the right evidence, and make the final version feel specific but honest.

body.text: Tailoring a CV does not mean rewriting your whole career for every vacancy. It means changing the emphasis so the reader can see the match faster.
The common mistake is copying job-ad language into the CV without evidence. That can sound optimized, but it does not feel trustworthy. A tailored CV should still sound like you.

A repeatable customization process keeps this from becoming a rewrite every time. The before-and-after resume examples make the difference easier to see: the facts stay true, but the emphasis gets sharper.

body.heading: Match the job description with your real experience.
body.text: Paste the role, review suggested themes, and approve only the edits that are true for your background.
Read the job description in three passes

items: First pass: highlight the must-have requirements, such as tools, qualifications, years of experience, or clinical registration.
Second pass: highlight the work style, such as stakeholder management, fast-paced clinics, audit, leadership, or documentation.
Third pass: ignore generic words unless you can prove them with a specific example.

body.text: Build a small matching table

rows: Stakeholder management
Weekly status calls with client operations and finance teams
Summary and top experience bullets
Excel or reporting
Built a monthly dashboard used by department leads
Skills and one result-focused bullet
NHS documentation
Completed patient notes and escalation records under local protocol
Clinical experience section

body.text: If a requirement has no honest match, do not force it. You can still apply if the role is a stretch, but pretending creates a weak interview later.
Change these parts first

items: Summary: mention the role type and strongest matching evidence.
Skills: keep only skills you can defend with examples.
First three bullets: move the most relevant achievements up.
Job titles and headings: keep them accurate, but clarify context where needed.

body.text: A quick before and after

rows: Responsible for reporting and communicating with teams.
Prepared weekly pipeline reports for sales and operations leaders, highlighting blockers before Monday planning meetings.
Worked in a busy ward and supported patients.
Supported a high-turnover medical ward, maintaining patient observations, documentation, and timely escalation during shifts.

body.text: Before sending, check the ATS-friendly format guide so the tailored version is still easy to parse.
What not to change

Do not change facts to match the advert. Dates, job titles, qualifications, registration status, and employer names should stay accurate. Tailoring is about selection and clarity. If the employer wants Kubernetes and you have only used Docker, say Docker. If they ask for ICU and your experience is acute medicine, show acute medicine honestly.
Resume keywords belong inside real evidence. A keyword in the skills section is weaker than the same idea shown inside a truthful work example.
How to know the CV is tailored enough

Read the first half of page one without looking at the job ad. A recruiter should be able to tell what role you are applying for and why your background is relevant. If the answer could still be any job in your field, tailor the summary and top bullets again.

articles.title: How to Customize Your CV for Each Job Without Starting Over
articles.description: A fast, repeatable CV customization process for job seekers who need tailored applications without rewriting from scratch.

body.text: The fastest way to customize a CV is to stop treating every application like a blank page. Keep one strong base CV, then make a focused copy for each role.
A good customization pass usually takes less time than writing a cover letter, but it has to be deliberate. You are choosing evidence, not sprinkling keywords.

A vague base version slows everything down, so fix the resume writing first. Once the broad CV is solid, the generic CV vs tailored CV comparison shows what should change for a specific job description.

links.text: generic CV vs tailored CV comparison

body.text: Keep a base CV with more detail than you send

Your base CV can be longer and messier than the application version. Use it as a private source file: old projects, extra achievements, tools, courses, audits, publications, volunteer work, and examples you may need later.
Use a 20-minute customization pass

items: Minutes 1-5: read the job ad and mark the real requirements.
Minutes 6-10: choose the three strongest matches from your base CV.
Minutes 11-15: update the summary, skills, and first role bullets.
Minutes 16-20: cut weak or unrelated detail so the page stays readable.

body.text: Do not customize everything
Leave stable facts alone: job titles, dates, employer names, qualifications, registrations, and anything legal or credential-related. The parts that change are emphasis, order, examples, and wording.

rows: Summary and top skills
Employment dates
First 3-5 bullets under recent roles
Qualifications or professional registration
Project examples
Job titles unless you are clarifying scope
Order of sections
Achievements you cannot explain in an interview

body.text: Save each version clearly

Name files so you can find the exact version later: Firstname-Lastname-Company-Role-CV.pdf. If you get an interview, review that version before the call. It is easy to forget which evidence you emphasized.
Build a reusable evidence bank

After each application, keep any strong bullet you created. Over time you will build a bank of truthful examples for leadership, reporting, patient care, customer work, project delivery, or technical skill. That makes the next customization pass faster and usually better.
This also protects your voice. Instead of asking a tool to invent new wording each time, you are reusing examples that came from your real work and adjusting the emphasis for the next role.
For roles screened through a portal, custom wording still needs a clean ATS-friendly resume format so the evidence is easy to parse.
For a slower, more deliberate review, the job-description tailoring guide shows the full comparison. The resume checklist is better saved for the final pass, when the wording is already chosen.

body.heading: Create a focused version faster.
body.text: Use the job ad to choose the right themes, then keep the edits honest and reviewable.

articles.title: Generic CV vs Tailored CV: What Actually Changes
articles.description: See the difference between a generic CV and a tailored CV, with practical examples of what to change before applying.

body.text: A generic CV is not always bad. You need one as your base. The problem starts when that same broad version is sent to roles with different priorities.
A tailored CV does not invent experience. It changes the order, wording, and examples so the employer sees the relevant parts first.

The practical workflow is simple: tailor your CV to a job description, then use a faster customization pass once the target role is clear. That keeps the base CV intact while making the sent version feel intentional.

links.text: tailor your CV to a job description

body.text: What a generic CV usually does

items: Uses a summary that could fit many jobs.
Lists every skill with the same weight.
Keeps older or less relevant duties near the top.
Describes responsibilities but not why they matter for this role.

body.text: What a tailored CV does

items: Names the type of role and strongest matching evidence.
Moves relevant skills and achievements higher.
Cuts details that distract from the application.
Uses job-ad language only where it is accurate.

rows: Managed projects and worked with cross-functional teams.
Led onboarding projects with product, support, and sales teams, keeping launch tasks visible through weekly status reports.
Experienced healthcare professional with strong communication skills.
NMC-registered nurse with acute ward experience, confident in patient handovers, medication checks, and escalation documentation.
Used Microsoft Office and reporting tools.
Built Excel tracking sheets for monthly service reports and reduced manual follow-up with standardized inputs.

body.text: The tailored versions are not longer. They are more specific. That is why they feel more credible to a human reader.
Use the base CV as inventory

Keep your generic CV as a private inventory of everything you might use. The application version should be narrower. If the role is about operations, show operations. If it is about patient-facing care, show clinical examples. If it is about reporting, show reporting.
When a generic CV is still useful

A generic version is useful for recruiters who ask for a quick overview, networking conversations, and your own record keeping. It is also helpful when you are still deciding which direction to take. But once you choose a specific vacancy, the generic version should become the starting point, not the final attachment.

The more competitive or specialized the job is, the more obvious tailoring needs to be. A hiring manager for a clinical, technical, or regulated role is usually looking for evidence in a particular order, not a broad list of everything you have done.

Keywords still matter, but they should follow the evidence. When a role asks for reporting, clinical documentation, or stakeholder updates, show where you did that work instead of adding a loose keyword list.
The before-and-after resume examples show the same idea in real bullets, with weak claims rewritten into specific evidence.

body.heading: Replace broad claims with role-specific evidence.
body.text: Paste the job description and compare it with your CV before you apply.

articles.title: How to Write a Medical CV for UK and US Healthcare Jobs
articles.description: A concise medical CV guide covering clinical experience, registration, education, audits, teaching, research, and role-specific tailoring.

body.text: A medical CV has to do more than list jobs. It needs to show clinical safety, scope of practice, training, and evidence that you can work inside the system you are applying to.
The exact format changes between the UK and US, but the reader still wants the same thing: accurate credentials, relevant clinical experience, and a clear fit for the post.
The CV vs resume distinction matters most when you are crossing countries or systems. For nursing applications, the NHS nurse CV checklist gives a more role-specific version of the same principles.
Put credentials where they cannot be missed

items: Name and contact details.
Professional registration, such as GMC, NMC, HCPC, state license, or board eligibility where relevant.
Current role, grade, specialty, and main setting.
Right to work or visa status if the employer asks for it.
Key certifications such as ALS, BLS, ACLS, PALS, or specialty-specific training.

body.text: Choose sections based on the role

rows: NHS doctor post
GMC status, grade, rotations, audits, teaching, courses, portfolio evidence
Nursing role
NMC status, ward or specialty experience, medication safety, handovers, patient documentation
US clinical role
License, board status, residency or fellowship, procedural volume, patient population
Research or academic role
Publications, presentations, grants, methods, ethics approvals, teaching

body.text: Write clinical experience with scope
Avoid vague lines such as worked in a busy hospital. Say where you worked, who you cared for, what you were trusted to do, and how you communicated risk.

items: Managed daily reviews for acute medical patients under consultant supervision, including documentation, escalation, and discharge planning.
Supported post-operative patients on a surgical ward, monitoring observations and escalating deterioration according to local protocol.
Completed QI work on discharge summaries, auditing delays and presenting changes at departmental teaching.

body.text: UK and US differences

UK medical CVs often include clinical rotations, audits, teaching, courses, and portfolio activity. US applications may lean more on residency, fellowship, board status, publications, procedural exposure, and formal credentials. Do not send the same version to both without adjusting terminology.
Make dates, gaps, and scope easy to understand

Healthcare CVs are checked carefully. Keep dates consistent, explain career breaks briefly when needed, and avoid making a short observership sound like a full clinical post. If you changed country or system, make the transition clear so the reader understands what experience is directly transferable.

Scope matters too. A line about emergency medicine means more when it says whether you clerked patients, assisted procedures, completed documentation, joined ward rounds, or worked under direct supervision.
A medical CV can be detailed, but it still needs a final resume checklist for missing dates, unclear registration details, and formatting issues.
NHS-specific applications need the NHS medical CV guide's section order. Doctor roles can follow the doctor CV formula when clinical grade, audits, and teaching need to lead.

body.heading: Tailor your medical CV to the post.
body.text: Use the job description to choose the clinical evidence, courses, audits, and skills that matter for this application.

articles.title: NHS Medical CV Guide: What Recruiters Expect to See
articles.description: A practical NHS medical CV guide covering registration, clinical posts, audits, teaching, courses, and how to tailor evidence for UK roles.

body.text: An NHS medical CV should make eligibility and clinical fit obvious. Recruiters should not have to search for your GMC status, grade, recent posts, or evidence that you understand UK clinical practice.

Think of this as the NHS overview. A broader medical CV guide helps when you are moving between UK and US formats, while the NHS nurse CV checklist keeps nursing applications from being forced into doctor-focused sections.
Start with the essentials

items: GMC registration status and license to practise.
Current grade, specialty, and employment setting.
Right to work or visa information if requested.
ALS or other required certifications.
Availability, location preferences, or notice period when relevant.

body.text: Use a recruiter-friendly order

rows: Profile
2-3 lines on grade, specialty interest, NHS or equivalent experience
Registration and courses
GMC, ALS, specialty courses, mandatory training if relevant
Clinical experience
Recent posts, rotations, responsibilities, patient groups, supervision level
Audit and QI
Topic, your role, measurement, outcome, presentation
Teaching and leadership
Who you taught, format, feedback, rota or supervision responsibilities

body.text: Describe NHS fit, not just medical skill

Clinical skill matters, but NHS posts also look for documentation, escalation, multidisciplinary working, governance, patient safety, and communication. Add evidence for those areas when the job description asks for them.

items: Documented ward reviews, discharge summaries, and escalation plans under local policy.
Worked with nursing, pharmacy, therapy, and senior medical colleagues during ward rounds.
Presented audit findings at departmental teaching and agreed a change with the supervising consultant.

body.text: Keep audit and teaching specific

Do not just list audit titles. Say what problem you measured, what standard you used, what you changed, and whether you re-audited. For teaching, mention the audience and format, such as bedside teaching, simulation, induction, or exam preparation.
If your NHS experience is limited

Do not hide non-NHS experience. Translate it carefully. Show patient safety, escalation, documentation, team communication, and governance using plain wording. If you have observer, clinical attachment, or trust induction experience, label it accurately and explain what you learned about UK practice.
Recruiters understand that candidates arrive through different routes. What helps is a CV that makes your current readiness clear instead of overstating familiarity with the NHS.
After the NHS evidence is in the right order, a final checklist pass for dates, registration details, links, and formatting can catch small errors that distract from strong clinical experience.
If you are writing for a doctor role, the NHS doctor CV example has a tighter structure.

body.heading: Make your NHS evidence easier to scan.
body.text: Match your CV to the person specification and keep the final version clear, accurate, and recruiter-friendly.

articles.title: NHS Doctor CV Example: Sections, Skills and Structure
articles.description: A compact NHS doctor CV structure with examples for clinical experience, audits, teaching, leadership, and courses.

body.text: A doctor CV works best when it separates eligibility from evidence. Put registration, grade, and courses near the top, then use the rest of the CV to show clinical judgement, communication, teaching, and improvement work.

This structure is designed for NHS doctor applications. For roles outside the UK, compare it with the broader medical CV guide so the terminology, registration details, and section order match the employer.
Recommended section order

items: Name, contact details, GMC status.
Short profile naming grade, specialty interest, and strongest fit.
Certifications and courses, including ALS if required.
Clinical experience in reverse chronological order.
Audit, QI, research, publications, and presentations.
Teaching, leadership, management, and memberships.
Education and qualifications.

body.text: Example profile

GMC-registered doctor with acute medicine and emergency department experience, confident with ward reviews, patient documentation, escalation, and multidisciplinary handovers. Interested in clinical governance and service improvement, with recent audit work on discharge summary quality.
Write clinical bullets with context

rows: Worked in acute medicine and saw patients.
Reviewed acute medical admissions under senior supervision, documenting plans, requesting investigations, and escalating deterioration.
Did audits and teaching.
Completed an audit of discharge summary delays, presented findings at departmental teaching, and helped introduce a ward checklist.
Good team player.
Coordinated with nursing, pharmacy, and therapy teams during ward rounds to support discharge planning.

body.text: What to tailor for each post

For a trust-grade medicine post, move acute ward experience and ALS higher. For a teaching-heavy role, bring teaching and feedback forward. For a specialty training application, show portfolio evidence, QI, publications, presentations, and specialty commitment.
Do not let extra sections crowd out clinical fit

Doctors often have many possible sections: prizes, conferences, memberships, electives, leadership roles, courses, and publications. Include them, but keep the strongest clinical evidence easy to find. A recruiter should not have to pass three pages of extras before seeing your recent post and responsibilities.
If an achievement is old or only loosely related, shorten it to one line. Save the detail for applications where that evidence is central.

Before exporting, check the basics: dates are consistent, GMC status is accurate, courses are current, and each section supports the post. The resume checklist is not medical-specific, but it is useful for avoidable mistakes.
The NHS medical CV guide covers the wider structure around registration, training, governance, and UK terminology.

body.heading: Build a doctor CV around the post.
body.text: Use the person specification to decide which clinical, audit, and teaching evidence should lead.

articles.title: NHS Nurse CV Checklist: What to Include Before Applying
articles.description: A focused checklist for NHS nurse CVs, covering NMC registration, clinical areas, medication safety, handovers, documentation, and tailoring.

body.text: An NHS nurse CV should make your registration, clinical setting, and patient safety experience clear within the first page. The reader needs to know where you have worked, what care you provided, and whether your experience matches the post.

This checklist is for nursing roles. A wider healthcare structure belongs in the medical CV guide, and the CV vs resume guide is useful when you are comparing UK terminology with US-style applications.
Top-of-CV checks

items: NMC registration status is visible.
Current or most recent ward, department, or specialty is clear.
Band level, preceptorship, or senior responsibilities are accurate.
Required courses or competencies are included if relevant.
Contact details are written as text and easy to copy.

body.text: Clinical experience checks

items: Patient group and setting are specific, such as acute medicine, surgical ward, ED, community, mental health, or ICU.
Medication safety, observations, escalation, and documentation are described with examples.
Handovers, MDT communication, and discharge planning appear where relevant.
Leadership duties, student support, link nurse roles, or shift coordination are not buried.

body.text: Example bullet rewrites

rows: Worked on a busy ward and cared for patients.
Provided care on a 28-bed acute medical ward, monitoring observations, updating care plans, and escalating deterioration to senior staff.
Good communication and teamwork.
Prepared structured handovers for day and night teams, highlighting medication changes, mobility risks, and discharge actions.

body.text: Tailor to the person specification

If the post emphasizes safeguarding, show safeguarding training and relevant experience. If it emphasizes acute assessment, move triage, escalation, and observation examples higher. If it asks for leadership, show coordination, mentoring, audits, or link nurse work.
The broader NHS medical CV guide is a useful cross-check against the person specification, especially for evidence around documentation, escalation, governance, and multidisciplinary working.
Adjust emphasis by band

For Band 5 roles, make clinical foundations, safe practice, documentation, and learning clear. For Band 6 roles, add evidence of shift coordination, mentoring, escalation decisions, audits, service improvement, or acting-up responsibilities where accurate.
The same experience can be framed differently. A medication round may show safe practice for one role, while coordinating medication safety checks across a team may support a more senior application.
A quick resume checklist pass can catch missing contact details, inconsistent dates, broken links, and formatting issues that weaken an otherwise strong NHS nurse CV.

body.heading: Check your nurse CV against the role.
body.text: Use the person specification to choose the right examples before you apply.

articles.title: What Is an ATS Resume? A Plain-English Guide
articles.description: Learn what an ATS resume is, how applicant tracking systems read applications, and how to keep your CV readable for software and people.

body.text: An ATS resume is a resume that an applicant tracking system can read without losing important details. It is not a special secret format. It is a normal, well-structured document with clear headings, real text, and relevant wording.

ATS software helps employers collect applications, store candidate information, and search or filter submissions. A recruiter may still read your CV, so the goal is not to write for software only. The goal is to avoid formatting and wording that hides your fit.
Once the basics are clear, the ATS resume template gives you a clean structure and the resume checklist helps catch problems while you are editing a real document.
What ATS software usually reads

items: Contact details.
Section headings.
Job titles and employers.
Dates.
Skills, tools, qualifications, and keywords.
Education, certifications, and licenses.

body.text: What causes problems

items: Important text placed only inside images.
Complex tables, columns, text boxes, or graphics.
Creative section names that make parsing harder.
Keyword stuffing that makes the CV unreadable.
Missing exact terms from the job description when they are true for you.

body.text: ATS-friendly does not mean boring

A clean CV can still have strong writing. The best ATS-friendly resumes use simple structure and specific evidence. They do not rely on decoration to look good, and they do not repeat the same keyword ten times.
Be careful with ATS score myths

Some tools sell a single ATS score, but employers use different systems and settings. A score can be a useful prompt, not a guarantee. The better test is whether the CV uses the employer's important terms naturally, keeps facts easy to parse, and remains readable to a person.

A stronger ATS resume usually improves two things at once: the layout is easy to parse, and the wording reflects the job description without sounding copied. That is why format and keywords should be checked together.

rows: Keyword list: project management, project management, project management.
Led a 6-week onboarding project with support, sales, and product teams.
A designed skill chart with icons only.
Skills: Salesforce, Excel reporting, customer onboarding, renewal tracking.

body.text: Formatting and wording usually fail together: the ATS-friendly resume format guide deals with layout, while the resume keywords guide keeps role terms tied to evidence.

body.heading: Check your CV against the job description.
body.text: Find the role-specific terms that are true for your experience and add them naturally.

articles.title: ATS-Friendly Resume Format: Simple Layout Rules That Work
articles.description: A clear ATS-friendly resume format guide covering headings, columns, file types, fonts, and layout choices that help recruiters read your CV.

body.text: An ATS-friendly format is mostly about restraint. Use a layout that keeps the text in a logical order, so both software and recruiters can follow the story without guessing.
For the basics, the plain-English ATS resume guide explains what the software is trying to read. This page is the layout pass: headings, dates, columns, and file type.
Use familiar section headings

items: Summary
Skills
Work Experience
Education
Certifications
Projects
Publications or Research, if relevant

body.text: Creative headings such as My Journey or Things I Do Well may look friendly, but they make the page harder to scan. Simple headings are not a weakness.
Keep layout predictable

rows: One main text column
Multiple narrow columns with mixed reading order
Standard fonts such as Arial, Calibri, Helvetica, Times, or similar
Decorative fonts that are hard to read
Plain bullets
Icons as the only label for important information
PDF when accepted, DOCX when the employer asks for it
Image-only files or locked designs

body.text: Make dates and titles easy to connect
Each role should clearly show job title, employer, location if useful, and dates. Do not separate dates so far from the role that a reader has to match them manually.
Do not hide contact details
Put your email, phone, city, and links in normal text. Avoid placing contact information only in a header image, graphic, or icon row.
A simple structure

items: Name and contact details.
Two-line summary.
Skills grouped by type if the list is long.
Experience with 3-5 useful bullets per recent role.
Education, certifications, and extras.

body.text: If you still want a designed CV

Use design to support reading, not replace it. Subtle spacing, clear hierarchy, and a calm accent color are usually fine. Problems start when the design changes the reading order, hides labels, or turns important details into graphics that cannot be selected as text.
If you are applying through a portal, choose the simpler version. If a recruiter asks for a portfolio-style PDF later, you can share a more visual version as a supplement.
Small layout mistakes can make an ATS-friendly resume harder to scan. The resume checklist is a useful last pass for broken links, unclear dates, and inconsistent section labels.
A ready-made ATS resume template can hold the structure, but the resume keywords guide is where the wording gets matched to the role.

body.heading: Check format and wording together.
body.text: A readable layout works best when the content is also matched to the job description.

articles.title: Resume Keywords for ATS: How to Match a Job Description Naturally
articles.description: Learn how to choose resume keywords from a job description and use them naturally in your summary, skills, and experience bullets.

body.text: Resume keywords are the words an employer uses for the experience, tools, qualifications, and responsibilities they care about. They help ATS software and recruiters connect your CV to the role.
The mistake is treating keywords like a shopping list. A keyword only helps if it is true and attached to evidence.
The ATS resume guide explains the screening basics. Strong wording still needs an ATS-friendly resume format so the keywords sit inside a clean, readable layout.
Find the useful keywords

items: Job titles and role names.
Tools and systems, such as Salesforce, Excel, Epic, or Jira.
Credentials, licenses, or registrations.
Core tasks, such as triage, reporting, stakeholder management, or discharge planning.
Industry terms that describe the work accurately.

body.text: Ignore empty keywords

Words like motivated, dynamic, fast-paced, and excellent communicator are usually too vague on their own. If the job ad asks for communication, show the setting: handovers, client calls, stakeholder updates, patient education, or written reports.
Place keywords where they make sense

rows: Summary
Mention the role type and 1-2 strongest matching areas.
Skills
List tools, methods, and credentials you can explain.
Experience
Use keywords inside real achievements or duties.
Certifications
Use the exact official name where possible.

body.text: Example

If the job asks for CRM reporting, stakeholder updates, and onboarding, do not write a skills line that simply repeats those words. Write a bullet like: Built weekly CRM reports for onboarding managers and shared status updates with sales and support leads.
Keep the human reader in mind
A recruiter can tell when a CV is stuffed with search terms. Read the final version aloud. If it sounds unnatural, remove repeated terms and keep the strongest evidence.
Use exact terms only when they are true

If the advert says account management and your role used the phrase client success, you can include both if they describe the same work. If the advert says Python and you only used spreadsheets, do not add Python. Keyword matching is useful only when it makes the CV more accurate.

A good final pass is to highlight every keyword you added. For each one, point to the bullet, project, role, course, or certification that proves it. If you cannot point to evidence, remove the keyword.
A job-description tailoring guide is useful when those keywords need to become a focused summary, skills section, and first-page evidence.

body.heading: Find the keywords that match your real experience.
body.text: Compare your CV with a job description and choose the terms you can honestly support.

articles.title: Resume Checklist: 25 Things to Fix Before You Apply
articles.description: A practical resume checklist for content, formatting, ATS readability, tailoring, proofing, and final export before sending an application.

body.text: Use this checklist after you have written the CV, not before. At this stage the goal is not to keep adding content. It is to remove friction before a recruiter or hiring system reads it.
Work through it once for your base CV, then again for each job-specific version. The second pass should be faster because you are checking fit, not rebuilding the whole document.
A base version that still feels thin needs better resume writing first. A clean draft can come straight here for the final quality check before export.
Content

items: The summary names the role type and strongest relevant evidence.
The first page shows the most important experience for this job.
Every recent role has clear scope, not just duties.
Bullet points include outcomes, scale, tools, or context where useful.
Skills are backed by experience elsewhere in the CV.
Old or unrelated detail has been shortened.
No claim would be hard to explain in an interview.

body.text: Tailoring

items: The CV reflects the job description without copying it blindly.
Must-have qualifications or registrations are easy to find.
Relevant tools, systems, and methods use the employer's wording when accurate.
The top bullets match the role's main priorities.
Generic phrases have been replaced with specific examples.

body.text: Format and ATS readability

items: Contact details are plain text.
Headings are simple and recognizable.
The layout has a clear reading order.
No important information appears only in images or icons.
Dates, employers, and job titles are easy to match.
The file type follows the employer's instructions.
Links work and point to updated profiles or portfolios.

body.text: Proofing

items: Spelling, grammar, and punctuation have been checked.
Tense is consistent for current and past roles.
Formatting is consistent across dates, bullets, and headings.
There are no duplicate bullets saying the same thing.
The final filename is professional and specific.
You saved the exact version you sent.

body.text: A CV that fails the format section needs an ATS-friendly resume format pass before it is ready to send.
Wording problems usually belong in the resume keywords guide. Structure problems are easier to spot against the ATS resume template.
When to stop editing

Stop when the CV is clear, accurate, and matched to the role. Endless editing can make the document stiff. If you have checked the facts, fixed the first page, and removed vague claims, send the application and keep a copy of the version you used.

body.heading: Run a final role-specific check.
body.text: Compare your CV with the job description before you send the application.

articles.title: ATS Resume Template: A Simple Structure You Can Use Today
articles.description: A clean ATS resume template with section order, example wording, and practical notes for keeping the document easy to scan.

body.text: This template is intentionally simple. It is designed for applications where clarity matters more than decoration: ATS parsing, recruiter scanning, and easy tailoring.
The ATS resume guide explains the concept; this page gives you the structure. Copy the order into your own document, then use the resume checklist before you apply.
Template

items: Name | Email | Phone | City | LinkedIn or portfolio
Professional Summary: 2-3 lines focused on role fit.
Skills: grouped tools, methods, clinical areas, or technical strengths.
Work Experience: job title, employer, dates, then 3-5 bullets for recent roles.
Education: degree, institution, dates or graduation year.
Certifications: licenses, courses, registrations, or credentials.
Projects, publications, audits, or volunteering if relevant.

body.text: Example summary

Operations coordinator with 5 years of experience supporting customer onboarding, reporting, and cross-functional project tracking. Strong background in Excel dashboards, CRM data quality, and weekly stakeholder updates.
Example experience block
Operations Coordinator, Northline Software, 2023-Present

items: Maintained onboarding trackers for 40+ active customer accounts, flagging blockers before weekly implementation calls.
Built Excel reporting templates used by support and sales teams to monitor renewal risk.
Updated CRM records after customer meetings, improving handover quality between sales and onboarding.

body.text: Template rules

A good ATS resume template should feel plain in the right way. The value is not visual novelty; it is a section order that lets a recruiter find job titles, dates, skills, and evidence without effort.

rows: Use one main column
The reading order stays clear.
Keep headings plain
Recruiters and ATS software recognize the sections.
Write bullets as evidence
The CV feels specific instead of generic.
Tailor before exporting
The final version matches the role, not every possible job.

body.text: Adapt the template by seniority

Early-career candidates can include projects, internships, coursework, volunteering, and placements if they prove relevant skills. Senior candidates should spend less space on early education and more on recent scope, leadership, commercial impact, clinical responsibility, or technical ownership.
For medical or regulated roles, add registration and mandatory credentials near the top. For technical roles, group tools by type so the skills section does not become an unreadable list.

For job-specific applications, update the template after reading the job description. The same layout can support different versions as long as the summary, skills, and first bullets change with the role.
The ATS-friendly resume format guide covers the layout details. The resume keywords guide covers where role language belongs inside the template.

body.heading: Use the template with a real job description.
body.text: A simple structure works best when the content is matched to the role.

articles.title: Before and After Resume Examples: Generic CV vs Tailored CV
articles.description: See practical before-and-after resume examples that replace generic wording with clearer, job-specific evidence.

body.text: The biggest difference between a generic CV and a tailored CV is not length. It is specificity. The tailored version gives the reader a reason to believe the claim.
Weak bullets need better resume writing before they need optimization. Once the base is clear, the customization guide helps decide which examples should move higher for a specific job description.
Example 1: customer operations

rows: Responsible for customer onboarding and internal communication.
Coordinated onboarding for 25+ new accounts, sharing weekly status updates with sales, support, and implementation teams.
Good at reporting and problem solving.
Built a renewal-risk tracker in Excel that helped managers spot delayed setup tasks before customer check-ins.

body.text: Example 2: nursing

rows: Worked with patients on a busy ward.
Provided care on a 30-bed acute ward, monitoring observations, updating care plans, and escalating deterioration according to protocol.
Strong communication skills.
Prepared structured handovers for incoming teams, highlighting medication changes, falls risk, and discharge actions.

body.text: Example 3: early-career project work

rows: Helped with university projects and teamwork.
Led a 4-person research project, setting weekly tasks and presenting findings to a class of 40 students.
Used data analysis tools.
Cleaned survey data in Excel and created charts used in the final presentation.

body.text: How to rewrite your own bullets

items: Replace responsible for with the action you actually took.
Add scope: number of users, patients, accounts, reports, shifts, or projects.
Name the tool, setting, or audience when it matters.
Connect the bullet to the job description without copying it.

body.text: Why the after versions work
Each after version gives the reader context. It names the setting, the action, and the reason the work mattered. That is why it feels more human than a polished but generic statement.

You can use the same approach in a summary. Instead of saying experienced professional with strong communication skills, say who you worked with, what kind of communication you handled, and what role you are targeting.
When you add keywords, keep them inside the evidence. A bullet that says CRM reporting, stakeholder updates, and onboarding all in context is stronger than a keyword list with no proof.
The generic CV vs tailored CV guide explains the strategic difference; the tailoring guide turns that difference into an application-by-application workflow.

links.text: generic CV vs tailored CV guide

body.heading: Turn vague bullets into evidence.
body.text: Compare your CV with a role and choose the examples that make the strongest match.
